Client 2: Yeah, and just… plain English. Like, what happens if the market drops, and are we still okay month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Joseph, I’ll start broad: what’s the household gross income per year, roughly, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, household gross is 162100 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not missing anything—Joseph, that includes your self-employed business income plus any salary pieces and investment income, and Jennife, your W-2 pay as well?
Client 1: Yes—my mix is a little messy: I’ve got monthly salary of 14450 a year, then an ad hoc salary piece of 47550, business income of 69350, and interest and dividends of 30750. Jennife’s is the rest.

Client 2: Mine’s the steady paycheck—39,550 a year. I don’t track the other buckets like he does.
Advisor: That’s helpful. Switching to the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, groceries, insurance, the whole thing.
Client 2: We’re pretty consistent—6450 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And within that, do you have any monthly debt payments—like a mortgage payment, car loans, student loans, credit cards—anything that’s a required payment each month?
Client 2: No, none. Monthly debt payments are 0.

Client 2: Yeah, and just… plain English. Like, what happens if the market drops, and are we still okay month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Joseph, I’ll start broad: what’s the household gross income per year, roughly, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, household gross is 162100 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not missing anything—Joseph, that includes your self-employed business income plus any salary pieces and investment income, and Jennife, your W-2 pay as well?
Client 1: Yes—my mix is a little messy: I’ve got monthly salary of 14450 a year, then an ad hoc salary piece of 47550, business income of 69350, and interest and dividends of 30750. Jennife’s is the rest.

Client 1: Correct—loans outstanding are $0.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk about what you’ve built up. Roughly, what are your investable assets total—so retirement accounts, brokerage, cash-like accounts, anything invested—excluding the house value for a moment?
Client 1: Investable assets total are 966050.
Advisor: Thanks—hang on, I’m just noting that down. And then separately, you do own your home—what’s the current value you’re using for the primary residence?
Client 1: We use 865450 for the house value.

Client 1: Investable assets total are 966050.
Advisor: Thanks—hang on, I’m just noting that down. And then separately, you do own your home—what’s the current value you’re using for the primary residence?
Client 1: We use 865450 for the house value.
Advisor: Okay. Now I’m going to ask this in a slightly annoying way, just to map the accounts: what are the main buckets making up that invested total?
Client 1: There’s a joint taxable account—well, it’s held through an insurance company platform—value is 543800.

Client 2: Yeah, and just… plain English. Like, what happens if the market drops, and are we still okay month to month.

Advisor: Okay. Next one’s about dependents—kids or anyone financially dependent on you. How many dependents should I put down right now?
Client 2: None—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And then, on how we’ll work together—do you want this relationship set up through my firm as an RIA, a registered investment advisor, versus, say, a brokerage-only arrangement?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just writing that down.

Client 2: Yep.
Advisor: Perfect—thanks for confirming all the debt pieces. Let’s switch gears for a minute to a couple of household basics that affect how I set things up for you, Joseph and Jennife.
Advisor: First, just to confirm—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And country-wise—are we planning everything as a US household, or is there any other country residency I should be aware of?

Advisor: Hi Joseph, hi Jennife—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d say a win is getting everything organized—income, what we’ve got invested, and making sure we’re not taking risks we don’t understand.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… plain English. Like, what happens if the market drops, and are we still okay month to month.

Client 1: We’re in TX.
Advisor: Got it—Texas. And on taxes, what bracket do you generally think you’re in these days? I know it can move around year to year.
Client 2: I think we’re in the 35%.
Advisor: Okay, 35%—thank you. And just so I’m matching the plan to your comfort level: when markets get choppy, do you two lean more cautious, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 1: Conservative. I’d rather give up some upside than take a big drawdown.

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Two more household basics, Joseph and Jennife, and then we’ll get into how that affects the plan.
Advisor: First, what state are you resident in for tax purposes—where you file from most of the time?
Client 1: We’re in TX.
Advisor: Got it—Texas. And on taxes, what bracket do you generally think you’re in these days? I know it can move around year to year.
Client 2: I think we’re in the 35%.

Advisor: Okay. That helps me understand the structure. And just to close the loop: no other debts, and no life insurance or disability policies you’re actively relying on right now?
Client 2: No, we don’t have any policies like that at the moment.
Advisor: Understood. Last thing for today’s baseline—your overall comfort level sounds conservative. Is that fair to say for both of you?
Client 1: Yes, conservative.
Client 2: Definitely conservative—sleep-at-night matters.

Client 1: It’s 122550 gross for the year.
Advisor: Thanks—one sec, I’m just noting that down. And that 122550 is your gross annual number, not net take-home, correct?
Client 2: I always mix that up—gross is before taxes, right?
